content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Best stoner comedies?

**Score:** 35
**Body:** Movies like Fast times at Ridgemont High, Dude where’s my car?, Harold and Kumar, how high.

Need something to laugh at. 

Also could be movies like scary movie which isn’t really a stoner comedy but has some stoners in it. 

Give me your best stoner comedy classics!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Grandma's Boy
  - Sorry I came on your mom. 
2. Half Baked (1997 or 98) is an obvious one here.
3. Anything by Cheech and Chong
  - The scene in up n smoke when they’re in the car after smoking is peak stoner comedy. 
4. The Stoned Age (1994)
5. Pineapple Express is my favorite 
6. Pineapple Express (2008) classic stoner movie, you've probably seen it but wasn't mentioned in your post

Smiley Face (2007) silly stoner comedy

Pizza Movie (2026) silly and low stakes movie about a new research chemical, not amazing but a good time

Walk Hard: The Dewey Cox Story (2007) not a stoner movie but hilarious, goofy parody movie. There are definitely some drug montages that might scratch that itch for you
7. Grandma's Boy, Idle Hands, Cheech and Chong, Half Baked.
8. You gotta watch *Friday* (1995) which is light and fucking hilarious and also, because the main character is a stoner, *The Big Lebowsky* (1998). 

## 2. Name a film which has lesser known creature from folklore

**Score:** 31
**Body:** I recently watched a couple of films, Hokum (2026) and His House (2020). I really enjoyed them, but I found myself after both films going down an internet rabbit hole, looking into the history of the creature (minor spoiler alert : The cailleach in Hokum and the apeth in His House).

Can anyone recommend some more films that feature more obscure creatures from folklore around the world that I can sink my teeth into to scratch this new itch ? Thank you

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ravenous 1999, Consume 2017: Wendigo 

Clearcut: Wisakedjak

Exhuma: >!Yokai!<

Mama Wata: Nigerian lady with powers I don't know the subtitles were busted.

The Empty Man: >!Tulpa!<

Uncle Boonmee Who Can Recall His Past Lives and The Medium: Thai folklore. 

Also The Lure does The Little Mermaid properly. 
2. Tumbbad (2018), an Indian folk horror and a cult favourite.
3. I'm not quite sure if it's actual Irish folklore, but these are great to watch...by Tomm Moore  
Wolfwalkers (2020)  
The Secret of Kells (2009)  
Song of the Sea (2014)

Also.. Scottish folklore  
Beowulf (2007)
4. For non horror, try the old James Stewart film Harvey - whose title character is a púca!
5. Not movies, but 3 different tv shows explore various folklore beasties in a great way:

Grimm

Lost Girl 

Supernatural
6. Not a movie and it’s animated, but it’s such an underrated gem. “Fright Krewe” on Hulu has many of the Louisiana and bayou folklore creatures. 
7. Border (2018). Wonderfully weird movie about a woman who works as an airport security officer with a strange ability to smell fear, whose involvement in a police investigation leads to a stunning revelation about her heritage.
8. The Ritual (2017) features an entity from Norse mythology called a jotin. Well worth a watch.
9. For Chinese folklore: Journey to the West: Conquering Demons (2013)

10. Not a film, but Trese on Netflix is a comic-based series based around Filipino folklore and cryptids.

The Hallow is a horror movie set in Ireland about a family who moves deep into the back country for the father’s job doing eco research.



## 3. Crazy Ending

**Score:** 26
**Body:** I am looking for movies with craaazy endings. Like mind-blowing, jaw dropping, wtf did I just watch endings. 

I loved:

Uncut gems

Gone Girl

The Usual Suspects

The Others

The Mist

Any genre will do. Drama, thriller, horror(my fav), comedy, documentary.

I'd like to be stunned into silence. Preferably horribly. Please help and thanks in advance 😃

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Bugonia (2025)
2. *Sorry to Bother You*
 
and 

*They Cloned Tyrone*
3. The cabin in the woods 
4. Saltburn

Blowout

Memento

The Game
5. No Way Out

And apparently there are people out there who haven’t seen The Sixth Sense and don’t know the ending, so if you are one of those people, go watch now and don’t google anything. 
6. The Substance. Very crazy, very horrible ending.
7. Arlington Road

Oldboy
8. Melancholia
9. Fight club 

Drag me to hell

Enemy (2013)
10. 2001

Vanilla Sky

Monty Python and the Holy Grail

The Midnight Meat Train

Seven

Oldboy (see the original)

## 4. I want to see a movie about burnout

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I have been feeling burnt out for many years, Need a movie where the whole premise is around burn out and burnt out ppl. I really need to see how to cope with it and move ahead.

Don't worry Im taking medical help, this is not a substitute for it. I just want some motivation, real life example if possible, so that it can aid me to come out of this situation.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Office Space
2. Falling Down 
  - Maybe he shouldn't watch this one :)
3. Fight Club
4. American Beauty is JUST WHAT YOU REQUESTED!!

Or even darker is a movie called “Spun” with Brittany Murphy in it. Enjoy
5. Definitely Fight club 
6. Inside Llewyn Davis
7. I mean, any hospitality focused film sets that up to the max. Burnt 2015, boiling point 2021, the menu 2022.

Then you've got the outlier topping the list; Whiplash 2014
8. No Country for Old Men
9. Up in the Air 

## 5. Political Satire Movies

**Score:** 16
**Body:** I'm looking for movies that made fun of the times or of history to spark conversation or controversy. They make fun of evil and traumatic moments in history. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dr. Strangelove
  - Movie feels more relevant than ever. 
2. Wag The Dog
3. The Death of Stalin
4. In the Loop is pretty good 
5. Election. 

The Candidate. 

The Great Dictator. 

Being There. 

Jojo Rabbit. 
6. Bulworth (1998) Warren Beatty, Halle Berry

Head of State (2003) Chris Rock, Bernie Mac

HeadS of State (2025) John Cena, Idris Elba

Dave (1993) Kevin Kline, Sigourney Weaver

Man of the Year (2006) Robin Williams, Christopher Walken
7. Thank You for Smoking
8. Monty Python movies
9. Team America: World Police

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Director Bong Joon-ho believed that the real life killer would watch the film.

**Score:** 912
**Body:** 🎬 Memories of Murder (2003)

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The real killer did watch the film. Lee Choon-jae was caught in 2019 through DNA, and he said he felt nothing- "no feeling or emotion" after watching the movie.
  - Apparently he was in prison for another murder when he saw it. 
    - IIRC they caught the killer after he killed his sister-in-law.
2. The real life killer did watch the movie. Dude didn’t give a flying fuck about it. No emotion, no remorse, nothing. In a way, he might’ve been honored to be the subject of a critically lauded movie and maybe even chuckled deep inside that the director’s purpose hasn’t been fulfilled

Definitely a great movie though
  - Why would anyone expect him to respond to a movie when he obviously ignored his victims cries? To think that a movie would make a difference is laughable. 
3. For context : In the final scene of Memories of Murder (2003), Detective Park gazes directly into the camera, as if staring into the eyes of the killer.

Director Bong Joon-ho included this moment with the belief that the real murderer might one day watch the film.

In 2019, after decades of uncertainty, DNA evidence identified Lee Choon-jae as the perpetrator behind the Hwaseong serial murders. During his trial, Lee admitted with chilling detachment: "I just watched it as a movie. I had no feeling or emotion."

He had sat in the dark, watching his own crimes unfold on screen completely devoid of remorse. What was once a symbolic stare from Park becomes, in retrospect, a direct confrontation. It is a moment where fiction meets reality, and where cinema dares to look evil in the eye.
  - lol when cinema dared to look evil in the eye jeez get over yourself 

![gif](giphy|GCIztZ4yY3EzF0Q3yU)
  - Still, it seems a little overblown. There was no real confrontation. The killer had no reaction and felt nothing as one would expect from someone that could commit the crimes depicted.

If anything, I imagine the killer was either just treating it like fiction or simply thinking, "that part was not entirely correct." or "it didn't really happen like that." Criticizing minor details with no sense of remorse or recrimination.

Which in a sense is a more poetic effect. When the detective looks directly into the camera, we men in the audience are having the same reaction that the actual murderer had when he saw the film.
  - Did you use AI to write this summary?
4. Supposedly he didn’t care much about it, but after some time he watched it over and over.

## 2. What is that one dialogue or word from a movie that cracks you every single time even on just thinking?

**Score:** 661
**Body:** What is that one dialogue or word from a movie that cracks you every single time even on just thinking? 

Mine is Ben Stiller yelling policia in Ron Burgundy 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He’s losing his mind…and I’m reaping the benefits

![gif](giphy|dFf0MERFSuocE)
2. The Naked Gun (1988)

https://i.redd.it/n3nhsf2ztg2h1.gif

![gif](giphy|l9jiNVVkdsG4M)
4. "You know, morons."
5. "What do you mean, you people?"
6. I am serious. And don’t call me Shirley. 
7. "Smokey, this is not Nam' this is bowling, there are rules." 
8. Wee-Oou-Wee-Oou-Wee-Oou…
  - THAT'S A LOTTA NUTS!!!
9. Mer man....mer man

![gif](giphy|uE4gVmbjaZmmY)

## 3. ‘The Odyssey’ Star Lupita Nyong’o Responds to Racist Casting Backlash: ‘This Is a Mythological Story’

**Score:** 586

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|Q09lToTa0H3Es)


  - Clearly you’re not a golfer
2. The funniest thing about all this is all the people pretending that they’ve read The Odyssey. 
  - I was supposed to read it in high school, so it basically counts 
3. My only counter-argument is this: ALL the casting is wrong.

Matt Damon is a pasty Irishman. Ann Hathaway. Tom Holland. Robert Pattinson... they are all ghostly white.

Nobody actually looks Mediterranean. You know... dark tanned skin. Jet black, curly hair. Short-ish. 

Like an Ancient Greek or Trojan (Turk) might look.

Black casting is no better or worse, just, extremely wrong in the other direction. 
  - My main issue with this film is that Greece is one of the most colourful looking places on planet earth and Nolan is going to do his usual gritty, de-saturated thing and ruin it. 

It's going to be difficult to suspend my disbelief when he makes the place look like a November in Scotland. 
  - Did you just call Ancient Trojans 'Turks' lmfao
  - The Trojans wouldn't be Turks.  Turks didn't show up in the area until the medieval ages.  They're actually from Central Asia. 
  - Coward casting directors know that Stavros Halkias should have played Odysseus. 
  - >Nobody actually looks Mediterranean. You know... dark tanned skin. Jet black, curly hair. Short-ish

I am Mediterranean and we have a broad variety of different phenotypes here. Dark tanned skin sounds like bullshit

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Ms .45 (1981) is a film I am apprehensive about recommending but it deserves attention

**Score:** 54
**Body:** Ms .45. This movie demands your attention and it’s very in your face. It’s about a woman who is sexually assaulted horribly, curses the existence of all men so she buys a colt 45 and starts killing them randomly. I was recommended it to me because I said I love Mandy. I get the recommendation because revenge but these are totally different movies.

The feel is gritty New York if you like the Warriors and the Night of the Juggler this movie is for you. 

I have to warn you the sexual assault is brutal. It is plural as well.

This is a movie that you can’t just have on in the background. If someone walked in while you were watching it they would ask you “what the fuck”.

This movie is like Joker for women.

This is the kind of movie that I was apprehensive about recommending to you guys but it’s too unique for you to miss.

I really have to warn you before your viewing, the sexual assault is rough. It’s a serious film where you will feel different after watching it.

It is a horror thriller drama there is no comedy here.

It is about a persons reaction to violent sexual assault but it’s more tasteful than other movies tackling the subject. I can’t blame her. This is the world women are forced to live in, being treated like creatures. Oggled over and degraded. She gets her revenge for living in our society. The movie is the reaction to that trauma.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It is shocking how well the subject matter is handled by Abel Ferrara. Lund’s performance in the film is incredible. (She also helped write it!) It’s a favorite of mine, and yes it’s a hard film to recommend. I generally hate the “rape/revenge” genre but this one somehow avoids feeling exploitive for the most part. For most films in the genre the film is about revenge on the perpetrators, but this happens within the first fifteen minutes. Instead it’s about a woman pushed to the brink by a misogynistic society and her pushing back. But it’s not triumphant, it’s tragic.

 Also the soundtrack is bonkers good.  

  - You put this so well, better than I could express in articulacy. Thank you for your contribution to this thread.
2. I had a different reaction to the rape scenes as you did. I was kind of dreading them but was relieved to find that they weren’t nearly as bad or exploitative as I thought. They were quick and not overly dramatic and felt like they were done as the bare minimum to get the plot point across. 


For comparison, I’ve seen stuff like Irreversible and I Spit on Your Grave which scenes are much longer and much more intense/graphic.   Ms. 45’s scenes were much more lighter in comparison.  
  - Yeah. The worst scenes in this film are much closer to an after school tv special than they are to those movies. I get it at some level - if you’ve never seen a rape scene in a movie, well here you go. But if you have, this is nothing, cannot agree with OP calling it “brutal” at all. 

Which, on that note, I’m not entirely sure what the purpose of this film is. It just feels like a much more sanitized copycat of the revenge exploitation films of the 70’s. It breaks no new ground and certainly doesn’t have the impact of something like Spit or Last House. Which is probably why its legacy is nowhere near as important. 

Quite frankly the most interesting thing about this film IMO was the way it captured the everyday feel of NY at the time.
    - We discuss theater here that’s what it is no one is debating the accuracy of the science in the Matrix.

I emphasized the rape scenes because I am a victim of rape. Someone like me might not want to be exposed to the thoughts that the words invoke.

But that’s what this is, life, art it’s subjective. Something that affected me not be that for you because we are different person place and time. This movie and my life experience affected me, it didn’t for you and that is okay.
3. I definitely feel you on this one. I finally saw this like three years ago or so and I loooooved it but yeah, not one I'd recommend to just anyone. A very moving and engrossing film. And the performance from Zoe Tamerlis Lund is amazing. It's wild that, in real life, she was open about her heroin use and a real pro-heroin advocate. Plus a very talented musician and she went by quite a few names. Seems like an extremely imaginative, talented, intelligent, and troubled soul.
  - Lund also co-wrote Bad Lieutenant with her boyfriend at the time. It's another legitimately incredible Abel Ferrara film and might just be his masterpiece 
4. That 1981 brought this film, and Possession. wow.

>She gets her revenge for living in our society.

Is this something you got directly from the film, or just a larger interpretation you are making about misogyny? I mean...do you take this to be the specific message of the film?  

## 2. Thoughts on David Cronenberg's THE BROOD (1979)

**Score:** 26
**Body:** \-I recently watched this movie for the first time and then read reviews from when it was released. I can't believe the disrespect it got from critics, but I'm guessing because it came out at around the same time as a lot of brainless slasher and creature features, this was a case of being genre-film ghetto-ized.  
  
That's a shame because THE BROOD has some actual substance. True, it features killer dwarves and body horror straight out of ALIEN (1979), but there's also larger themes like divorce and all of the ugliness that can create (literally, in reference to the aforementioned killer dwarves).  
  
\-One of the main criticisms I read about the film was the supposedly ugly nature of the violence. It's true that for the most part, these scenes go on long enough that they seem to be happening in real time, which might add a certain verisimilitude some viewers would find disturbing. At the same time, however, perhaps Cronenberg wanted them this way so we'd have no choice but to take them seriously.  
  
This is strictly armchair psychology on my part, but my understanding is that the inspiration for THE BROOD was Cronenberg's own difficult divorce around the time of FAST COMPANY (1979). Doubtless, that period in his life was one of great mental and emotional stress, so perhaps the violent scenes in THE BROOD represented the director projecting his own feelings about how the divorce process left him bloody and broken (figuratively).  
  
\-Even in hindsight, I find the character of Nola, the maternal figure played by Samantha Eggar, endlessly fascinating from a psychological standpoint. What does it say that in her rage, she manifests creatures who resemble cracked-mirror versions of her own daughter Candice? What does it say that the "brood"-lings don't appear not to have capacity to love anyone back, including Nola, yet she seems to care more for them than for Candice?  
  
Also, if the "brood"-lings are born of rage, why don't any of them ever resemble Nola's own parents, whom she has legitimate beef with? If the creatures are meant to be the objects of her visualized anger, why is her anger so acutely directed at her own kid?  
  
\-Having now watched SHIVERS, RABID, and THE BROOD, I've enjoyed seeing the evolution of the "evil scientist" character. In Cronenberg's earlier two movies, they were either entirely devoid of personality or seemingly oblivious to the consequences of their actions, but Oliver Reed's Dr. Raglan is noticeably different. He may start out in the film as an amoral figure, but he seemingly has that elusive moment of conscience before the end.  
  
\-The strong technical aspects of Cronenberg's movies continued with Mark Irwin serving as his cinematographer for the second time. There are scenes involving the brood-lings invading a kitchen as well as a classroom that yield genuinely disturbing moments, in no small part because these spaces as photographed (and through production design too, I'm sure) felt either clean and downright antiseptic, or warm and inviting. This makes the inevitable clash with the presence of the chaotic brood-lings all the more powerful.  
  
\-Overall, I appreciated that the movie was trying to say something about not just divorce, but generational trauma; specifically, that those who are victims of it are in danger of perpetrating it on their own children. Exactly how to prevent such a vicious cycle from perpetuating is something I didn't glean THE BROOD, but perhaps there no easy answers. Maybe the best way forward is to just be aware it's a thing.  
  
Next up to watch: SCANNERS (1981).

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I would agree with you on this one.  I think on the surface The Brood is slightly ill paced but when you dig into the psychology of it becomes quite interesting.  I listened to a commentary on the Second Sightt 4k disc by a film historian that specializes in Croneberg, William Beard.  He points out all the little bits of psychology from Freud and Jung.  When they visit the wife's room the pictures on the wall Freud and Jung.  This is a movie that had me research more about psychoanalysis, and both Freud and Jung schools of psychology.

I know some say that a film should be able to stand on its own, and the film on a surface level is a good almost B movie quality movie.  But if you learn about those psychological ideas of  time there is much more than meets the eye.

Of his early works I prefer this over Shivers and Rabid.
  - Thanks! As far as where I rank THE BROOD amidst Cronenberg's films up to that point, I also put it above SHIVERS and RABID (and definitely above FAST COMPANY).
  - >  I think on the surface The Brood is slightly ill paced but when you dig into the psychology of it becomes quite interesting.

For me, a lot of Cronenberg is better recollected than experienced. He makes movies that I may not want to re-watch often, but they're something I carry with me forever. The Brood is a peak example of this.
2. Here’s the thing: You have to look at the reviews in the context of when the movie was originally released.

I recall that time (*Cough-old-fart-cough*) and *The Brood* was seemingly *so far out there* and so genuinely grotesque -the idea of these bizarre “little children” killing people in incredibly bloody ways!- that audiences were indeed turned off by the material.

So the passage of time, as it often does with action or horror films, tends to blunt what was once super cutting edge “nastiness” (as perceived by viewers at the time) and we can subsequently focus more on the story told.  And it remains a disturbing one, for sure, but not as disturbing as to make one want to walk out of theaters or dismiss the film offhand as just being “nasty” for nasty sake.

To put it in another way: 1979 was easily one of the wildest years for horror film releases.  Also released that year was *Alien* and *Phantasm*, two films which also made me literally shake in the cinema when I originally saw them.  Perhaps a decade or so later *Alien* was shown on regular TV pretty much uncut and all those incredible scares had been seen so many times they no longer had the same shock value.  It even got to the point where Mel Brooks, in *Spaceballs*, parodied what was one of *Alien’s* greatest shocks (you know what I’m talking about!).

But *Alien* was basically a science fictional film without these heavier themes in it and was a hit.  *Phantasm* did have themes of loss but it too didn’t tread on issues like *The Brood* did.

Another example is John Carpenter’s *The Thing*.  When that film was released, critics and audiences *did not* care for it.  In part they were -perhaps- basking in the glow of *E.T. The Extraterrestrial*, which featured a far more benevolent alien encounter, and were disturbed by this very graphic horror film.

The good thing is that over time -as I noted- the in-your-face horror that might have been too much for audiences to take becomes less and one can see this films without the disturbing elements blinding you from the stories told.  It doesn’t help the people who made the film -*The Thing* was a huge box office flop and Carpenter found himself making films on a smaller and smaller budget before essentially giving up entirely- but it does show that films can be reassessed… even if it is much after they were originally released.
  - Thanks for your points! It's great that the film has been reassessed more positively all these decades(!) later. I agree that it makes sense for the reasons you mentioned. The violence may have been outdone by various other movies since, but the story remains intelligent and rooted in something we can understand on a base level.


If released today, it would probably fit in nicely with the current trend of elevated horror.
3. The Brood is my favorite Cronenberg, which is mostly due to Oliver Reed’s performance. From the opening scene of him playing his patient’s withholding father I knew this was gonna be a classic. It also has such a Canadian winter coziness thing happening in the compound, particularly with Reed’s outfits. Psychologically too I can’t think of another divorce movie which goes to these extremes. 
4. Other than it being a kind of attack on his (ex)wife during a custody battle, I loved it. She was a kind of Alien Queen. The horrible special effects were kind of amazing. A lot on the cult-California subculture was interesting, and the versions of masculinity put in juxtaposition to a kind of female Goddess (archetype). The movie really stayed with me.
5. Birth and very young children have plenty of potential for what we now call ‘body horror.’  

I recently rewatched this one for the first time since 1979 in a theater about a year ago.  The segment that was most unsettling to old me, now, was >! the classroom killing.  Staging a gory monster attack in an environment strongly coded as safe for children is as primally disturbing as the shower scene in *Psycho*, and the defenseless innocence of elementary schoolers makes it even more so.!<  Somehow, in 1979, Cronenberg was able to intuit a horror that became all-too real in future decades, and depict that onscreen.

Oliver Reed is terrific.  Despite portraying a fairly unsympathetic guru, he makes him comprehensible.

And even though little people in monster masks creates the potential for inadvertent physical comedy, Cronenberg keeps the proceedings tense and disturbing.  
6. One fun little detail I saw mentioned in an old documentary or interview about this movie is the fact that Nola is in a seated position in every single scene she’s in. She never stands up. This was deliberate. She’s literally “brooding” - as in incubating - at all times. 

## 3. Blue Spring (2001) is one of the best representation of depression in Teenagers I've ever seen

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I recently rewatched this film for a second time and I gotta say it holds up on rewatch even better than I expected, in fact I gave it higher rating than on my first watch. it's a very intricately crafted film that has intentional subtle allegories and symbolism that I didn't catch first time. here is my in length review from letterboxd that is my interpretation of this film, it's  little messy I had to get my thoughts out quickly so I wrote it at 3 am lol but I stand by this review and would like to hear your thoughts what you think about this film.

spoilers ahead, please be warned, if you haven't watched it I can't recommend it enough

no other movie has shown depressive and hopelessness state and bleakness of what future holds for youth as well as blue spring did. you have all these characters that at one glance seem very different from one another but most of them have one thing in common, they are lost, unguided and completely doomed to fail in life.  
"what do you wanna become when you grow up ?" the most recurring and worn-out but nevertheless relevant question one can ask to children comes up in this movie quite a lot and I think it's trying to highlight the pressure that's placed on young children to constantly deliver in life, whether it's exams, baseball or anything else.Toyoda is trying to make us sympathize with the characters with this statement and I'd be lying if I said it's not working.

on the rewatch I'm starting to see all these little details and symbolism at work that imo makes this movie stand out. for example Kujo is someone that's unafraid of death and completely lost the plot in life ,literally. he's just as directionless as all the other kids but he's the one that has nothing to lose, that's why in the beginning he wins that clap contest which in the result crowns him as a boss. he has pretty much everything kid his age at that school could have ever wanted but he's still depressed and unhappy. the reason being he has no idea what he wants do, this is also juxtaposed by baseball guy that goes around running on the field screaming "Akira high let's go, let's go" and some other dude with caterpillars, about which Kujo says "People who know what they want... they scare me". this aimless and purposeless mindset drives these characters to the edge of collapse, Kujo is just someone that has nothing to lose and in the absence of fear that's where he draws his confidence and mental strength from, that's the sole reason he became boss of the gang.

you have Aoki on the other hand that has not completely lost it yet, because he has Kujo, he looks up to him, Kujo is his existential compass that guides him to be someone he needs to be in order to be his worthy sidekick. but when Kujo rejects him, he too morphs into someone that has nothing to lose and is just as aimless and purposeless as Kujo. this proves my point in last scene when we see that painting that Kujo draw in the beginning of the film, now Aoki did the same painting. I think it's an indication of Aoki becoming the same type of person Kujo was when he first won that clap contest, his main goal becomes to surpass Kujo, hence the final clap contest scene.

in the last scene we see Kujo's shadow next to that giant thing that's drawn on the roof and it's one of the best closing shots I've ever seen. in this last shot we see Kujo's shadow getting dwarfed by this big giant shadow and I think it signifies that despondency, dejection and unknown state of future rules over this characters and their lives and it completely drives them to do all this violent acts towards each other. they are driven by instinct to do harm to person, objects and to themselves, it's a very hyperbolic expression of depression but make no mistake, Blue Spring delivers one of the most impactful statement amidst similar contemporary films.

side note: I noticed this one interesting detail, there is one guy in a class who's constantly sleeping. so during classes, lunchtimes and breaks he's still sleeping and when school's over he runs out the gate and off he goes, he's rushing, he's rushing to get somewhere, but where ?.  
I mean think about it, the guy sleeps all day and when he wakes up everything is over, he missed his favorite class, he missed the chance to talk to the girl he liked or to a friend he wanted to have. I think Toyoda being genius he is, he's using this character to tell us, we let time pass by and when we move we're always behind the schedule, we always try to race against the clock not knowing we didn't even start the race..

## 4. Does anyone have experience working with small/local theaters for event screenings or features?

**Score:** 6
**Body:** This is a very niche question, but given that this is a more niche subreddit I wanted to see if anyone has experience, advice, or just general information around working with theaters on private showings as a film enthusiast.

For context, my wife is big into old-school horror (Romero/Lloyd Kaufman/anything John Carpenter, anything with remotely similar vibes). She's also into lost films, the Muppets, and generally anything in an equally eclectic range.

She has expressed interest in running screening series where she can find fun intersectionalities (i.e. "women in horror who love big hairy monsters" and it's all just bigfoot horror with female directors, as a hypothetical) and also just share that interest of weird old movies with people who might also be interested.

I want to help her work with local theaters to potentially make this happen, and I work in financial risk management so I'm generally pretty good at talking to people and having my shit together. However, I (presumably) cannot merely acquire a 35mm copy of Who Framed Roger Rabbit, rent out a theater with a 35mm projector, and go nuts with my wife and 30 other people before we follow it up with a showing of The Descent.

TL;DR: Does anyone here with experience actually screening movies have a short list of important things I need to know before I ask a local theater owner if my wife and I can show weird movies to other people on a regular basis and talk about them in their theater?

To be clear, because my wife told me I should say so, it's important to note that these films are films that have been shown in theaters before and aren't (that) weird or (at all) pornographic.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It’s difficult to just get an older film that you want to screen, played at a local theater. The theater has to acquire rights to screen it, and that’s more expensive than a single screening of enthusiasts. The theaters near me that show older films, generally do themed months where they’ll focus on a particular actor/director/genre for a month. I’m not familiar with any theater which will take requests like that though. To put in perspective how difficult it is to screen movies, I was in a horror club in college, and we were only allowed to screen movies in the public domain, and this was a free club with no affiliation to a theater, which will undoubtedly have much higher standards for legality. Even if you own physical copies of the films or even a film reel, that doesn’t mean you have the right to publicly screen it.
  - My local theatre has single day showings of older releases daily.  Viva las Vegas,Party Girl,  Menace to Society,  Hapiness, off the top of my head.  There's also showings for course work in college programs that are free. They show about 4 movies that are currently at theatres. Guy I met shows bad obscure movies every month,  like Bat Pussy, first parody porno considered the worst ever made.  Large Popcorn and large drink are less than 10 bucks. Beer and a shot special for 5 bucks.   I believe they can do this because they are a non profit theatre.  
    - Does the guy run his club through the theater? If so that’s awesome! I do have a few non-profit cinematheques in my city and they do play a lot of excellent older films. They’re the only theaters I really go to, because I like to support them and because I’m much more interested in the films they screen than the big name theater chains. I’m not sure if my locals would do requests though. Also, even if they did, if OP is looking to screen bigger name movies like The Muppets or The Descent, it’s gonna be a bit more complicated/expensive than something like Bat Pussy, which you have me very interested in looking into lolol. I really wish my film club could meet in a theater and watch together, instead we all watch the films in our own time and then meet at a bar.
      - They're showing Blue Velvet, Fight Club, 8 mile this month.  They range from obscure to art house,  to old blockbusters.
https://kankanindy.com/
2. Venues be unable able to screen a film without a screening licence.  

I run a community cinema in a small town in the UK and and also on the board of a community cinema and arts centre in a nearby city.

In my opinion your first step would be to design your programme of films. This would involve finding the rights holder for each film and obtaining a financial quote for the screening rights licence for the film. 

The next step would be to approach theatres with a view to coming to an agreement about screening your films, how to manage ticket sales and marketing of the events. 

In my opinion, If you were able to put some "skin in the game" by offering to pay up-front for the screening licences then you'd have a basis to start a negotiation.
3. The difficulty of this is going to vary greatly based on where you are. There's nothing stopping you from renting out a theater and print for a private showing. The hard part is that 35mm prints are big and heavy and expensive to ship. You'll need to find a 35mm projector available for rent (not extremely rare) and a protectionist who you'll need to pay to work the screening (not rare in big cities).

If you're in NYC or LA or London it's not too bad. If you're in Fairbanks, Alaska it might be impossible. 

If you're in LA I can point you in the right direction.
  - Exactly this\^. People will say you need licenses for a theater to show a film. That’s true, in a sense. In a different sense, it’s equally true that nothing is stopping you from renting out a theater once a month for a private showing of whatever you want and having people pay $5 a month to be in your film club (the theater manager might stop it, but a local one by me is happy to play these sorts of “under the table” movies). You legally cannot explicitly sell a ticket to the movie, and you probably can’t advertise what the movie is on any official channels (ie the theater can’t put Roger Rabbit on their website, you could probably make a Facebook page for your film club and say you’re showing Roger Rabbit).

You will need to lose money on it, going this route. You’re footing the bill upfront and you can politely ask for donations but you should expect to lose money on this venture. Which some people are fine with!
4. I am a non-professional freelance programmer (e.g. side gig) in a major city, and I work with an independent cinema to screen films in a format similar to what you're talking about. You mention "private showings"; if you're talking about truly screening films *privately* (i.e. renting the venue and not charging admission to attendees), you can probably get away with simply screening things from a disc or whatever digital format is compatible with the cinema's projection setup.

However, if you're talking about doing these screenings publicly and charging for tickets, it's a bit more complicated. Like others are telling you, there's an upfront cost associated with securing the rights to screen a film publicly (which varies from film to film), and generally an independent cinema that operates, you know, as a cinema will want to be confident that people beyond you and your wife will want to pay for them, especially if you're talking about actual film, which requires transportation as well as rights.

If your wife wants to cold pitch her idea to cinemas that work with external freelance programmers, that would be a good place to start. When I put my series together I essentially sent out a one-page pitch outlining my curatorial angle, a list of sample films, and a general sense of what gap my idea was filling in the city's existing film programming. I had no other credentials, but that got me into the room and I've now been doing it for several years.

As an alternative, look into what microcinema or pop-up cinema spaces exist in your city, because there may be some that are willing to be a little bit more loosey-goosey with the rules of exhibition simply because they're such small potatoes that nobody is paying attention, and screening from a Blu-Ray or something would be within bounds to save money on acquiring a DCP. If you're going for niche and really community-oriented, this could be your best bet.

## 5. The Illusionist did the crowned prince dirty

**Score:** 3
**Body:** I just rewatched the Illusionist because I remember really liking it the first time around. I think I probably saw it when it first came out in 2006.

What occurred to me this time that didn't the last time was that the crowned prince is actually not the villain! Yes, he was a womanizer and a treasonous asshole (and possibly a murderer?), but he didn't kill the Dutchess!

Ed Norton and the Dutchess totally stitch him up for her "murder" and the guy ends up killing himself because of it, all the while beforehand expressing to anyone who will listen that Eisenheim is a fraud and is manipulating the situation, which he was!! 

AND, when Paul Giamatti puts all of this together in the final scene, he's smiling as though he's just so chuffed that they managed to trick him...but he literally just watched the crowned prince shoot himself because he was framed....

Does no one else see this as whacked? The guy was just trying to create a better Prussia or whatever, and totally gets the shit end of the stick.
